front: Photograph: shows a boy (Colin Wood) in Central Park with one suspender strap hanging off his shoulder, making a grimace, clutching a fake MK2 Pineapple grenade in his claw-like hand
back: Child with Toy Hand Grenade in Central Park (Diane Arbus)
work: Child with Toy Hand Grenade in Central Park (1962)

front: Artist: Norman Mailer made a hostile comment about 'giving grenades to babies' after she photographed him sprawled awkwardly in an armchair for the New York Times Book Review
work: Child with Toy Hand Grenade in Central Park (1962)

front: Photograph: shows twin girls Cathleen and Colleen Wade in matching corduroy dresses and headbands with opposite expressions; directly inspired the ghost twins in The Shining
back: Identical Twins, Roselle, New Jersey, 1967 (Diane Arbus)

front: Photograph: shows the nearly nine-foot-tall 'Jewish Giant' Eddie Carmel leaning down so his head doesn't hit the ceiling while his parents look up at him in their Bronx home
back: A Jewish Giant at Home with His Parents (Diane Arbus)
work: A Jewish Giant at Home with His Parents in the Bronx, N.Y.C. (1970)

front: Photograph: shows a young man in a suit and boater hat holding a small American flag and wearing a button reading 'Bomb Hanoi,' waiting to march in a pro-war parade
back: Boy with a Straw Hat Waiting to March in a Pro-War Parade (Diane Arbus)
work: Boy with a Straw Hat Waiting to March in a Pro-War Parade (1967)

front: Artist: switched from a grainy Nikon to a twin-lens Rolleiflex, resulting in her characteristic sharp, square-format photographs
Artist: Susan Sontag criticized her absence of beauty in On Photography, in the essay 'Freak Show' (also titled 'America, Seen Through Photographs, Darkly')
Artist: committed suicide in 1971; beforehand had written 'Last Supper' in her diary
Artist: former student Neil Selkirk compiled her 1972 retrospective and was the only person authorized to print her work
Artist: first major exhibition was 'New Documents,' which also included work by Lee Friedlander and Garry Winogrand
Artist: final series (posthumously titled 'Untitled') depicted residents at homes for the developmentally disabled, inspired by R.D. Laing's writings
Artist: published A Box of Ten Photographs — a limited edition of 50 sets with plastic boxes designed by Marvin Israel and handwritten captions; only 4 sold in her lifetime
Artist: photographed Anderson Cooper as a baby for a Harper's Bazaar Valentine's Day issue
